questions: Is `moderation_decisions` intended to be long-lived analytics (retained indefinitely) or operational (short TTL)? Assuming long-lived; no retention policy will be added.
Confirm the suspicious emoji set is exactly {🤮, 👎, 😭}, or should it also include 💩, 🙄, etc.? Proceeding with the three listed in the brief.
Should the admin DM include a jump link to the message, or just a plain text summary? Assuming jump link + reactor mention + emoji + content snippet.

Is there an existing workspace-level Supabase migrations repo I should check the exact filename-prefix convention for, or is the current `.migrations_staging/` timestamp format authoritative? Assuming the latter.

success_criteria.criterion: A new `moderation_decisions` migration SQL file exists under `.migrations_staging/` with columns for message/channel/guild IDs, reactor info, message author info, content snippet, classification (CHECK constraint covering the 4 values), reason, is_suspicious, removed_at, and appropriate indexes.
`DatabaseHandler.record_moderation_decision` exists in `src/common/db_handler.py`, is gated via `_gate_check`, and inserts into the `moderation_decisions` table.
The restricted-emoji branch in `reactor_cog.on_raw_reaction_add` records a `bot_auto_restricted` moderation_decision with reason in {flag, political, religious} before returning.
`on_raw_reaction_remove` writes a `user_self_removal` decision when no bot-initiated marker is present, and is a no-op (for moderation_decisions) when the marker is present.
Self-removal of 🤮, 👎, or 😭 sets `is_suspicious=true` and DMs `ADMIN_USER_ID` via the base_bot.py admin-DM pattern.
`on_raw_reaction_clear` and `on_raw_reaction_clear_emoji` listeners exist on ReactorCog and write `bot_clear_all` / `bot_clear_emoji` decisions respectively.
`tests/test_moderation_decisions.py` passes and covers the five scenarios listed in Step 4.2.
Full existing test suite (`pytest -q`) still passes with no regressions in reactor or logger tests.
No new config surfaces, env vars, or feature flags added beyond reusing existing ADMIN_USER_ID.
Message content snippet is truncated to 200 characters at the call site.
In-memory pending-removals set has opportunistic pruning (entries older than ~60s are dropped) to bound memory.
`_is_restricted_emoji` is refactored into `_classify_restricted_emoji` returning the category; boolean callers updated.
Manual smoke test in dev Discord: flag-emoji reaction → row with bot_auto_restricted; 🤮 self-retract → admin DM received.
Migration file is manually copied into `/Users/peteromalley/Documents/banodoco-workspace/supabase/migrations/` before deploy (handoff step, not automatable from the brain-of-bndc repo).

assumptions: `moderation_decisions` is a new net table, not a view over `discord_reaction_log`, because the log lacks classification, reason, message author, and content snippet — a view cannot synthesize those.
No backfill is required; the table tracks events forward-only from deploy time.

Single-reaction removals cannot be attributed to a moderator via Discord gateway events (payload.user_id is the reaction owner, not the actor). All non-bot-marked single removes are classified as `user_self_removal`. Moderator-driven removals are only distinguishable when they come through `reaction_clear` / `reaction_clear_emoji`.
Bot-initiated removals are tagged by registering (message_id, user_id, emoji) in a short-lived in-memory dict on the cog before calling `message.remove_reaction`; entries auto-prune after ~60s.
Suspicious emoji set is exactly {🤮, 👎, 😭} per the brief.
Admin DM reuses the pattern at `src/common/base_bot.py:176-183` (ADMIN_USER_ID env + fetch_user + create_dm); no new config.

Migration SQL is staged to `.migrations_staging/` with timestamp-prefixed filename, matching the existing handoff pattern, and must be manually copied into the workspace-level `supabase/migrations/` repo before deploy.

Writes are gated by the existing `_gate_check(guild_id)` path — if the table doesn't exist yet in prod the insert will error and be caught by the try/except; migration-first deploy order mitigates this.
Message content snippet = `message.content[:200]`; no sanitization beyond truncation.
Content cascading from message deletion is out of scope (no reliable single-event signal to distinguish it from user self-removal).
